title: Oracle Separation of Quantum Polynomial time and the Polynomial Hierarchy
speaker: Avishay Tal
abstract: In their seminal paper, Bennett, Bernstein, Brassard and Vazirani [SICOMP, 1997] showed that relative to an oracle, quantum algorithms are unable to solve NP-complete problems in sub-exponential time (i.e., that Grover's search is optimal in this setting).

proof_stages.label: Complexity-class landscape for the BQP versus PH question
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up P, NP, coNP, PH, PSPACE, BPP, and BQP, emphasizing that the true containments between efficient quantum computation and classical nondeterministic or hierarchical computation are unknown.

proof_stages.label: Oracle-relativized separation as the attainable target
proof_stages.summary: Since unrelativized separations would imply major open lower bounds, the speaker introduces oracle access, explains relativized complexity classes, reviews prior oracle separations, and formulates the goal of separating BQP from PH relative to an oracle.

proof_stages.label: Reduction to pseudorandomness against AC0
proof_stages.summary: The main theorem is restated through the Aaronson-Ambainis/Aaronson reduction: it suffices to construct a distribution on strings that fools constant-depth circuits but is distinguishable by a polylogarithmic-time quantum algorithm, using the standard translation between PH oracle computations and AC0 circuits.

proof_stages.label: Forrelation-based distribution
proof_stages.summary: The candidate distribution is explicitly defined by sampling a Gaussian vector, applying a Hadamard/Fourier transform to create weak cross-correlations, truncating, and then using the resulting coordinates as coin biases to obtain a Boolean distribution.

proof_stages.label: Quantum distinguishability of the distribution
proof_stages.summary: Using Aaronson's quantum algorithm, the acceptance probability is expressed through a forrelation statistic whose expectation is zero under the uniform distribution but about epsilon under the constructed distribution, giving inverse-logarithmic distinguishing advantage.

proof_stages.label: Sparse low-degree structure of shallow circuits
proof_stages.summary: The classical side is prepared by recalling AC0 circuits, their low-degree polynomial approximations, and the key refinement that AC0 approximants are sparse in each degree, unlike the dense quadratic statistic used by the quantum algorithm.

proof_stages.label: Fourier-extension reduction from Boolean to Gaussian analysis
proof_stages.summary: A Boolean function is expanded as a multilinear Fourier polynomial so that expectations over the rounded Boolean distribution can be compared, up to truncation error, with the value of the multilinear extension on the underlying Gaussian and at the origin.

proof_stages.label: Random-walk hybrid proof and parity-gate outlook
proof_stages.summary: The Gaussian is represented as the endpoint of many tiny independent Gaussian steps, a hybrid argument bounds each consecutive change using sparse low-degree approximation and the CHHL restriction lemma, and the talk closes by explaining that a stronger Fourier-coefficient bound would extend the method to AC0 with parity gates.
